role_summary: Chairman of the Student Nonviolent Coordinating Committee, 1961-1963; co-led the March 1960 Orangeburg student protest.
biographical_paragraph: In March 1960, on the campus of South Carolina State College in Orangeburg, Charles McDew and Thomas Gaither pulled roughly a thousand students into a coordinated protest against segregation [src: 2]. McDew was a Massillon, Ohio native, born June 23, 1938; he had taken his college years south, and the next year, 1961, he succeeded Marion Barry as the second chairman of the Student Nonviolent Coordinating Committee, holding the post until John Lewis took it in 1963 [src: 2]. He was arrested forty-three times in the course of that activism [src: 2]. Fellow organizer Bob Moses described him as "black by birth, a Jew by choice and a revolutionary by necessity"; McDew had converted to Judaism as a high school student, drawn there by the Talmud's writings on justice [src: 2]. Later he taught at Metropolitan State University in Minneapolis, with a focus on civil rights history and African American studies [src: 2]. He died April 3, 2018 in West Newton, Massachusetts, at seventy-nine [src: 2]; his Library of Congress oral history is entry 16 of the Civil Rights History Project [src: 1].

sources.title: Charles F. (Chuck) McDew oral history
sources.publisher: Civil Rights History Project, Library of Congress
